coredata.dc:title: Incorporating carbon quantum dots into phosphotungstic acid ionic liquid materials for enhanced catalytic oxidative desulfurization
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Jun
Huang, Haoxue
Zhang, Mei
Song, Pin
Li, Hongping
Di, Jun
coredata.dc:description: SOx emission from fossil fuel combustion is an important reason for environmental pollution. Achieving deep removal of sulfur-containing compounds from the fuel is essential. Herein, by combining phosphotungstic acid (HPW) with ionic liquids (ILs) and incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a series of catalysts (CQD/C18PWIL-x) for oxidative desulfurization (ODS) are prepared. Experimental results reveal that the modification of ILs could facilitate full contact between the active site and the organic phase, and improve mass transfer in the oxidative desulfurization process. Moreover, the incorporation of CQDs can promote the utilization of hydrogen peroxide. The synergistic action of multiple components leads to better activation of hydrogen peroxide. Therefore, the CQDs/C18PWIL-1 exhibits excellent desulfurization efficiency under ambient conditions, in which dibenzothiophene was completely removed within 30 min. Meanwhile, as heterogeneous catalyst, CQDs/C18PWIL-1 shows excellent cycling stability. The direct incorporation of CQDs significantly improves the catalytic activity and provides a new inspiration for the application of CQDs to ODS processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxidative desulfurization
Carbon quantum dots
Ionic liquid
Heterogeneous catalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations for C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-x: XRD spectra (a), FT-IR spectra (b) and Raman spectra (c), Steady-state fluorescence spectra (d) of C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-1.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a), band gap determination (b) of C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-x.
XPS spectra of C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-1: survey (a), W 4f (b), C 1 s (c) and O 1 s (d).
XPS VB spectra (a), and energy band structures (b) of C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-1.

The sulfur removal of DBT over C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-x (a), the sulfur removal of DBT over CQDs/C18PWIL-1 at different temperatures (b) and pseudo first order kinetic equation at correlated temperature(c-d). Experimental conditions: V (model oil) = 5 mL, m(catalyst) = 0.02 g, O/S = 5, T = 60 ℃.
Effect of aromatic compound on desulfurization efficiency. Experimental conditions: V (model oil) = 5 mL, m(catalyst) = 0.02 g, O/S = 5, T = 60 ℃, t = 30 min.
The desulfurization performance comparison of DBT, 4-MDBT and 4,6-DMDBT by using. Experimental conditions: V (model oil) = 5 mL, m(catalyst) = 0.02 g, O/S = 5, T = 60 ℃.

Recycle performance for the removal of DBT by using CQDs/C18PWIL-1 as catalyst (a), shot filtration test of CQDs/C18PWIL-1 (b). Experimental conditions: V (model oil) = 5 mL, m(catalyst) = 0.02 g, O/S = 5, T = 60 ℃.
Water droplets on the surface of catalyst C18PWIL (a) and CQDs/C18PWIL-1 (b), Oil droplets on the surface of catalyst C18PWIL (c) and CQDs/C18PWIL-1 (d).
Sulfur removal with different trapping agent by using CQDs/C18PWIL-1. Experimental conditions: V (model oil) = 5 mL, m(catalyst) = 0.02 g, O/S = 5, T = 60 ℃.
ESR spectra over C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-1. Experimental conditions: V (model oil) = 5 mL, m(catalyst) = 0.02 g, O/S = 5, T = 60 ℃.

GC–MS analysis of Oil phase before and after reaction (a) and extracted phase from the catalyst after reaction (b). Experimental conditions: V (model oil) = 5 mL, m(catalyst) = 0.02 g, O/S = 5, T = 60 ℃.

head.ce:title: Incorporating carbon quantum dots into phosphotungstic acid ionic liquid materials for enhanced catalytic oxidative desulfurization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, National Special Superfine Powder Engineering Research Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
National Special Superfine Powder Engineering Research Center
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, National Special Superfine Powder Engineering Research Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: SOx emission from fossil fuel combustion is an important reason for environmental pollution. Achieving deep removal of sulfur-containing compounds from the fuel is essential. Herein, by combining phosphotungstic acid (HPW) with ionic liquids (ILs) and incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a series of catalysts (CQD/C18PWIL-x) for oxidative desulfurization (ODS) are prepared. Experimental results reveal that the modification of ILs could facilitate full contact between the active site and the organic phase, and improve mass transfer in the oxidative desulfurization process. Moreover, the incorporation of CQDs can promote the utilization of hydrogen peroxide. The synergistic action of multiple components leads to better activation of hydrogen peroxide. Therefore, the CQDs/C18PWIL-1 exhibits excellent desulfurization efficiency under ambient conditions, in which dibenzothiophene was completely removed within 30 min. Meanwhile, as heterogeneous catalyst, CQDs/C18PWIL-1 shows excellent cycling stability. The direct incorporation of CQDs significantly improves the catalytic activity and provides a new inspiration for the application of CQDs to ODS processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of fossil fuels will remain the main source of addressing the world's energy demands, but the burning of fossil fuels leads to extensive SOx emission creating a range of environmental problems. To meet the energy needs of the public and the industrial sectors, it is necessary to minimize the sulfur compounds from fossil fuels.[1,2] At present, the desulfurization method widely used in industrial production is hydrodesulfurization (HDS). However, HDS technology requires high energy consumption and strict conditions to achieve deep desulfurization of fuel oil.[3,4] Thus, more environment friendly and efficient deep desulfurization technology should be explored to replace or complement HDS. In recent decades, non-hydrodesulfurization technologies, such as oxidative desulfurization (ODS), photocatalytic desulfurization (PDS), biological desulfurization (BDS), adsorptive desulfurization (ADS) and extractive desulfurization (EDS) have been widely studied.[5–10] Among them, ODS have emerged as powerful technology to complete the deep desulfurization process of aromatic sulfide, which has drawn particular attention.[11–15] The catalyst and the oxidant, as the two main aspects of ODS, directly determine the efficiency of ODS.

In the past few decades, heteropoly acid materials (HPAs) has developed rapidly in many catalytic fields.[16–19] Kegging-type HPAs, such as phosphotungstic acid (HPW), have been applied in aromatic sulfide removal. However, phosphotungstic acid has poor wettability in the organic phase, which can lead to poor dispersion in the organic phase. In the ODS process, it will limit the contact between the active site in the catalyst and the sulfur-containing substrate, further affecting the catalytic efficiency.[20,21] Another issue is that the activation capacity of hydrogen peroxide is low with phosphotungstic acid as catalyst for ODS. Therefore, sacrificial agents and higher hydrogen peroxide consumption are required.[22].

Ionic liquids (ILs) composed of organic cations and organic or inorganic anions have aroused tremendous attentions in many catalytic fields due to its easily adjustable structure, high thermal stability, and chemical stability.[23–25] Meanwhile, ILs have excellent wettability in organic phase. Dispersion of solid materials in ILs can significantly improve the contact between solid catalyst and substrate in organic phases.[26–28] Incorporating these characteristics of ILs into HPWs makes it possible to develop heterogeneous catalysts with high desulfurization performance for ODS. Herein, a simple ion exchange method was utilized to combine the octadecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (OTAC) with HPWs to prepare IL named C18PWIL. The dispersity of tungsten active site and the lipophilicity of the catalyst were improved. As a heterogeneous catalyst, C18PWIL not only have high activity in catalytic applications, but also exhibited excellent recyclability.

As an environmentally friendly oxidant, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been extensively studied due to its high reactivity and easy ease of handling.[29,30] H2O2 as oxidant has been widely used in ODS. However, it was found that due to self-decomposition severely, the activation capacity of hydrogen peroxide is low with phosphotungstic acid-based catalyst for ODS. Therefore, the improvement of H2O2 utilization efficiency in phosphotungstic acid-based catalyst is highly desired. New type of nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely studied due to their unique advantages such as easy synthesis, high temperature resistance, high plasticity, and excellent catalytic potential.[31,32] Importantly, it has been reported that the incorporation of CQDs into catalytic materials could effectively improve utilization efficiency of H2O2.[33–35] Based on that, we speculated that combining CQDs with C18PWIL catalyst could improve the utilization rate of H2O2 in ODS to achieve an efficient ODS process.

Herein, a series of catalysts (CQDs/C18PWIL-x for short) were prepared by incorporating different contents of CQDs into the initial catalyst C18PWIL and applied to ODS. Compared with the C18PWIL, CQDs/C18PWIL enhanced the ODS efficiency of the catalyst by promoting the formation of ·OH from H2O2. Subsequent experiments confirmed that CQDs/C18PWIL-1 exhibited higher desulfurization efficiency. With H2O2 as oxidant, CQDs/C18PWIL-1 can achieve deep desulfurization within 30 min at 60℃. Meanwhile, after 19 times of recycling, the catalyst still had high desulfurization efficiency, indicating the good stability.

The phosphotungstic acid (HPW) (H3PO4·12 (WO3), 99 %), octadearyl dimethyl ammonium chloride (OTAC) (C21H46ClN, 99.0 %), commercially available hydrogen peroxide (aqueous solution, 30 % H2O2), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, 98 %), citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents in the experiment can be used directly without additional purification procedures.

C18PWIL was synthesized by the ion exchange method. The specific process was as follows: 1 mmol of HPW and 3 mmol of OTAC were dissolved in 45 mL of deionized water to form a colorless transparent solution. After complete dissolution, the above solutions were mixed and the colorless solution immediately produced a white flocculent precipitate. Stirred vigorously for 12 h, centrifuged and washed with deionized water to neutral. Finally, the synthesized samples were dried in an oven at 60 ℃ for 12 h to obtain white solid powdery ionic liquid materials for subsequent synthesis and control experiments.

Dissolved 5 mmol of anhydrous citric acid in 10 mL of deionized water, added 335 µL of 1,2-ethylenediamine, and dissolved completely. The mixture was transferred into a 20 mL hydrothermal reactor at 200 ℃ for 5 h to obtain carbon dots solution.

0.1 g of C18PWIL and x mL of carbon dots solution (x = 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5) were dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water, stirred vigorously at a constant temperature of 60 ℃ for 12 h, centrifuged and washed twice with deionized water. Finally, the synthesized sample was placed in an oven and dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h to obtain a brown solid powder catalyst named CQDs/C18PWIL-x (x = 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5).

The preparation method of model oil was as follows: added a certain amount of DBT, 4-MDBT and 4,6-DMDBT to n-octane to simulate different aromatic sulfur compounds. The corresponding sulfur content was 500 mg/L. The oxidative desulfurization performance was tested in a set of bottles equipped with magnetic stirrer, condensing tube, and water bath heating. Under typical test conditions, 20 mg of catalyst and 5 mL of model oil were added to the reactor, and then kept at a constant temperature. After that, H2O2 was added to the reaction. The molar ratio of H2O2/ sulfur (O/S) is 5. The content change of DBT in the model oil was detected every 10 min. For research the effect of aromatic compounds on DBT naphthalene and p-xylene were added to the model fuel as competitive compounds, with content of DBT 500 mg/L S, and other conditions remained unchanged. After the reaction, the gas chromatography-flame ionization detector equipped with an FID detector was employed to detect the residual sulfur content in model fuel. After oxidative desulfurization reaction, the catalyst was recovered after drying at 60 ℃ and reused in the next ODS cycle.

The crystal structures of CQDs/C18PWIL-x and C18PWIL were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Fig. 1 a showed that the XRD pattern of prepared CQDs/C18PWIL-x was similar to C18PWIL. After introduction of ionic liquids, all catalysts showed the characteristics of amorphous peaks, the broad and weak diffraction peak at 15 − 35°, which demonstrated that the catalysts were typical amorphous carbonaceous materials.[36] No remarkable peaks of CQDs can be observed for CQDs/C18PWIL-x, revealing the good dispersion of CQDs in samples.[37] The results of XRD demonstrated that the incorporation of CQDs into C18PWIL does not destroy the basic structure of the catalyst.

The Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of CQDs/C18PWIL-x and C18PWIL are shown in Fig. 1b. The characteristic absorption bands for HPW Keggin anion structure were appeared at 1076, 972, 900, and 809 cm−1, which was contributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of P-O, W = O, W-Ob-W, and W-Oc-W, respectively.[38,39] The vibrational spectra confirmed that the Keggin anion structure of HPW was retained in the CQDs/C18PWIL-x, and the modification process of the CQDs did not change the basic structure of the catalyst.

The incorporation of the CQDs into C18PWIL were verified by the Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1c, in the typical catalyst CQDs/C18PWIL-1, the Raman spectra exhibited two characteristic peaks at 1310 and 1598 cm−1 were caused by the incorporation of CQDs, termed the D band (related to the disordered carbon structure) and G band (attributed to the graphitic domains in the structure), respectively. Compared with C18PWIL, the presence of G band and D band indicated the successfully incorporation of CQDs in CQDs/C18PWIL-1. Meanwhile, the intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) can reflect the disordered degree of the catalyst surface, the CQDs/C18PWIL-1 had an ID/IG of 1.04, indicating that there were a large number of disordered functional groups appear on the surface of CQDs/C18PWIL-1[40,41]. The results of Raman spectra indicated the successful combination of CQDs with the original catalyst.

In the steady-state fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1d), the spectral wavelength distribution had no obvious change. However, compared to C18PWIL, the fluorescence spectral intensity of CQDs/C18PWIL-1 was significantly reduced. The change in the steady-state fluorescence spectra meant that interactions between molecules were enhanced, which could enhance the electron transport process between CQDs and C18PWIL-1.[42] The enhanced electron transport process indicated that the catalytic reaction was easier to achieve, which facilitated the activation of H2O2, forming reactive oxygen species with strong oxidizing powers. The above characterization proves that CQDs have been successfully incorporated into the original catalyst.

SEM images (Figure S1) also demonstrated that the addition of CQDs made the overall structure of the catalyst more density. However, there was no significant changes in the microscopic characteristics of the catalyst. In the HRTEM images of the catalyst, no significant aggregation of related carbon materials was observed before and after the addition of CQDs (Figure S2 a-f). Additionally, the EDS elemental mapping images revealed the homogeneous distribution of C, P, O and W elements in CQDs/C18PWIL-1(Figure S2 g-k).

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) of samples were analyzed. From the spectra shown in Fig. 2 a, compared to C18PWIL, the absorption range of CQDs/C18PWIL-1 was extended to 400–800 nm, resulted from the modification effect of CQDs on the catalyst. The increase of absorbance in the visible-light area also proved that the CQDs was successfully incorporated into the catalyst. Meanwhile, compared to C18PWIL, the band gap values of CQDs/C18PWIL-1 was reduced (Fig. 2b). The reduced band gap values demonstrated that the incorporation of CQDs led to the electron excitation of catalyst in a lower energy range. This indicated that the catalytic reaction was more easily stimulated.[43] This change was more favorable for the catalytic oxidative desulfurization reaction.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to examine the chemical status of elements and electron migration of CQDs/C18PWIL-1. In the Fig. 3 a, the XPS results demonstrated the existence of C, O, W and P elements. By observing the high-resolution XPS spectra of W element in the Fig. 3b, the binding energy at 35.88 and 37.99 eV corresponded to W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2, respectively, which was consistent with the results of W6+.[44] CQDs/C18PWIL-1 showed a new characteristic peak of element C at a higher binding energy (292.3 eV), caused by the incorporation of CQDs (Fig. 3c).[45,46] Meanwhile, after engineering CQDs, the proportion of element C in CQDs/C18PWIL-1 increased (from 62.61 % to 69.41 %). In addition, the characteristic peaks of elements C, O and W of the catalyst incorporated by CQDs have shifted to lower binding energy, indicating that the addition of CQDs made the original elements C, O and W of the catalyst with more electron-enriched state, and the surface electronic density increased. The electron-rich catalyst enabled high-energy surface electrons being easily transferred to H2O2, which was favorable to break the O-O bond and induced the formation of active O species with strong oxidizing powers.[21] It further demonstrated that CQDs have been successfully incorporated, and existed a charge transfer process between catalyst ligands. The above results indicated that there was a strong interaction between the introduced CQDs and the original C18PWIL. The electronic properties were improved, which can contribute to improve ODS performance of the catalyst.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the XPS valence band (VB) spectra showed that the VB value of C18PWIL (3.81 eV) was higher than that of CQDs/C18PWIL-1 (2.82 eV). Combined with the estimated band gap energies of C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-1 in Fig. 2b (3.08 and 2.18 eV, respectively), the conduction band (CB) of C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-1 were calculated to be 0.73 and 0.64 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b). The lower CB level indicates that the introduction of CQDs significantly promotes charge separation, the W atom in the electron-rich catalyst can serve as an efficient active site for interacting with H2O2. The surface electrons of the catalyst are more easily transferred from the W atom to H2O2, which was favorable for the activation of H2O2 to form reactive O species, thus facilitating the ODS process.

The oxidative desulfurization experiment was performed under the same reaction conditions. The desulfurization efficiencies of samples with different CQDs contents are shown in Fig. 5 a. With the increase of CQDs content, the desulfurization efficiency of catalyst increased first and then decreased. Compared to C18PWIL, the incorporation of CQDs significantly improved the desulfurization efficiency of catalyst. It indicated that the incorporation of CQDs can promote the ODS process. It has been reported that CQDs can effectively improve utilization efficiency of H2O2, promoting the production of reactive O species during decomposition. [34] Among them, CQDs/C18PWIL-1 showed the best ODS efficiency, and the DBT was completely removed within 30 min at 60℃. This phenomenon means that CQDs accelerate the electron transfer from the active site to H2O2, resulting in the efficient production of ·OH. However, with the CQDs loading further increase, the desulfurization efficiency was reduced. On the one hand, it may be that excessive CQDs agglomerated and overlapped on the catalyst surface. The contact between the partial surface W-active site and H2O2 was affected. On the other hand, both amounts of CQDs and active sites have an impact on the efficiency of ODS. In the experimental system, the dosage of all catalysts was the same (0.02 g). But by analysing the XPS data of CQDs/C18PWIL-1, it can be seen that with the introduction of CQDs, the proportion of C element increases (from 62.61 % to 69.41 %) and the proportion of W element decreases (from 9.12 % to 6.45 %). Therefore, excessive CQDs can lead to the absence of the active site in the catalyst. These factors were not beneficial to the activation of H2O2.

Different temperatures of 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C, 70 °C and 80 °C were compared to discuss the effect of temperature on the ODS reaction of the typical catalyst CQDs/C18PWIL-1 (Fig. 5b). The reaction temperatures had influence on the desulfurization efficiency of ODS. Higher temperature would lead to rapid decomposition of H2O2, while lower temperature could affect the activation of H2O2. CQDs/C18PWIL-1 exhibited excellent ODS performance over a wide temperature range. When the temperature increased from 40 to 60 ℃, the desulfurization efficiency continuously enhanced. The desulfurization efficiency slightly decreased with further increase of temperature, and the desulfurization rate still can be up to 90 % when the temperature was 80 ℃. The reaction rate decreased when the temperature exceeded 60 ℃. This could be related to the decomposition of H2O2 during the reaction. To confirm the impact of the decomposition of H2O2, the ODS processes under different O/S molar ratios were studied. As presented in Fig. S4a, the desulfurization efficiency was gradually enhanced by increasing the O/S molar ratio from 3 to 5, which proved that a higher O/S molar ratio was conducive to improving the ODS efficiency. However, further increasing the molar ratio of O/S to 6, the desulfurization efficiency has slightly decreased. This may be caused by the fact that excessive H2O2 added into the reaction system produce more water, which restricted mass transfer and dispersion of the catalyst in the model oil. In addition, when the O/S molar ratio increased to 6, different temperatures of 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C, 70 °C were compared to be discussed the effect of temperature on the ODS reaction of CQDs/C18PWIL-1 (Fig. S4b), the reaction phenomenon was similar to that when the molar ratio of O/S is 5. The desulfurization efficiency slightly decreased when the temperature was 70 ℃, which may be due to higher temperature would lead to rapid decomposition of H2O2. It reduced the utilization of H2O2. The catalyst CQDs/C18PWIL-1 exhibited excellent desulfurization performance at different temperatures, proving that the incorporation of CQDs was conducive to the utilization and activation of hydrogen peroxide.

The pseudo-first-order dynamics model of ODS process related to temperature was further fitted (Fig. 5c-d). The corresponding fitting equation is as follows:[47].
(1)
∫
C 0
C
d C
C
= -
∫

k d t
(2)
ln
C
C 0
= - k t → C /
C 0
=
e
- k t
(3)
ln k =
- E a
R
1 T
+ ln A

The results showed that the ODS process conformed to the pseudo-first-order dynamics model. The pseudo-first-order kinetic equation can be used to calculate the activation energy (Ea) required for the reaction process. Ea of CQDs/C18PWIL-1 was 25.61 kJ/mol. Lower Ea indicated that the ODS process involving H2O2 and CQDs/C18PWIL-1 was more easily achieved.

Considering that the incorporation of CQDs affected the property of the catalyst, the wettability of the catalyst was also investigated (Fig. 9 ). The wettability of the catalyst was important for the ODS process. Good wettability facilitated full contact of the active site in the catalyst with the sulfur-containing substrate, which was beneficial for ODS efficiency. The results showed that when water droplets and oil droplets were dropped on the original C18PWIL and CQDs/C18PWIL-1, respectively. Both catalysts showed hydrophobicity and excellent lipophilicity. The results indicated that the modification of OTAC facilitates the dispersion of the catalyst in the model oil, ensuring full contact between the active site and the DBT. In addition, the lipophilicity of CQDs/C18PWIL-1 was a little improved in comparison to C18PWIL. It indicated that wettability was not main factor. The above results showed that with the incorporation of CQDs, the addition of CQDs increased the electron density on the catalyst surface, the lower CB position promoted the formation of more high-energy electrons. Meanwhile, the resulting high-energy electrons was more easily transferred to H2O2, which was important for improving the ability to activate H2O2 into reactive O species. Subsequently, CQDs improved the utilization of the catalyst for the generated reactive O species. And the good lipophilicity of C18PWIL was maintained, which ensured good dispersion of the catalyst in the model oil. The synergistic action of multiple components was favorable to the activation of H2O2, thus improving the ODS efficiency.

To verify the oxidation products of DBT, GC–MS analysis was conducted. In Fig. 12 a, it can be observed that the peak belonging to DBT (m/z = 184) in the original model oil disappears before the reaction, leaving only the strong peak of internal standard n-tetradecane. In Fig. 12b, a peak of DBTO2 (m/z = 216) can be observed after the reaction, these results indicated that DBT was ultimately oxidized to DBTO2. Based on the characterization, reactive O species and product analysis, a possible mechanism of DBT oxidation was proposed. In the ODS process, the W active site in HPW has electron transfer with H2O2. This enables to break the O-O bond, which causes the activation of H2O2 to form ·OH. In addition, CQDs have exceptional electron storage capacities and electron donating ability, which improves the electron surface density and electron transfer capacity of the catalyst. This capacity accelerates the electron transfer from the active site to H2O2 and further induces the efficient production of ·OH with strong oxidizing powers. The organic sulfur compounds are converted into their corresponding sulfoxides and/or sulfones by oxidation in the presence of oxidants, which can be removed through a simple extraction process.

In summary, a simple process was used to prepare CQDs modified phosphotungstic acid ionic liquid materials named CQD/C18PWIL. The characterization results indicated that the lipophilicity and dispersibility of the catalyst in the ODS process were improved by the incorporation of ionic liquid materials, and the utilization efficiency of H2O2 in ODS was further improved by the incorporation of CQDs, resulting in excellent oxidation desulfurization efficiency of the catalyst. The catalyst exhibited good catalytic ability, high selectivity, cyclicity, and can achieve 100 % removal both of DBT, 4-MDBT, and 4,6-DMDBT. In addition, as a heterogeneous catalyst, it was easy to recover and separate, which was conducive to ensuring fuel quality.
Jun Xiong: Writing – review & editing. Haoxue Huang: Investigation, Data curation. Mei Zhang: Investigation. Pin Song: Resources. Hongping Li: Resources. Jun Di: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Jiangsu Specially Appointed Professorship, Youth Talent Cultivation Plan of Jiangsu University, Natural Science Research Project for Universities in Anhui Province (KJ2021ZD0006), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (2208085MB21).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuel.2024.131168.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Confining polyoxometalate clusters into porous aromatic framework materials for catalytic desulfurization of dibenzothiophene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wang, L. Jiang, X. Zou, H. Tan, P. Zhang, J. Li, B. Liu, G. Zhu, Confining polyoxometalate clusters into porous aromatic framework materials for catalytic desulfurization of dibenzothiophene, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 12 (2020) 25910-25919.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Octamolybdates containing MoV and MoVI sites supported on mesoporous tin oxide for oxidative desulfurization of liquid fuels

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boric acid-based ternary deep eutectic solvent for extraction and oxidative desulfurization of diesel fuel
